Title: 200 homes razed in pre-dawn Sandakan fire
Article:
SANDAKAN: A pre-dawn fire destroyed about 200 homes in Kampung Bahagia here today.
The Sabah Fire and Rescue Department received a distress call at 1.32am and arrived at 1.48am after travelling 15km from the station.
The blaze destroyed non-permanent houses on water, covering an estimated 0.003ha.
In a statement, the department said narrow access routes, low tide, strong winds and closely built homes caused the fire to spread rapidly and hampered firefighting efforts.
The operation was also assisted by personnel from the Kinabatangan station, with water sourced from tankers and nearby hydrants.
A total of 35 personnel, along with agencies including Sabah Electricity, the Civil Defence Department and police, were involved.
No casualties have been reported, and operations are ongoing.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hizbollah rejects one-sided truce, vows response to Israel
Article:
BEIRUT: Hizbollah leader Naim Qassem said on Saturday that the ongoing 10-day truce with Israel cannot be one-sided, vowing that his fighters would respond to Israeli attacks on Lebanon.
"A ceasefire means a complete cessation of all hostilities. Because we do not trust this enemy, the resistance fighters will remain in the field with their hands on the trigger, and they will respond to violations accordingly," Qassem said in a statement read out on TV.
"There is no ceasefire from the side of the resistance only, it must be from both sides."
He also offered his first reaction since the ceasefire went into force at midnight on Thursday (2100 GMT), with the terms shared by the US State Department.
Qassem called it "an insult to our country and our homeland, Lebanon, that America dictates its text and speaks on behalf of the Lebanese government."
The truce came days after Lebanon and Israel's ambassadors to the US met in Washington ahead of planned direct negotiations between the two countries, the first in decades.
"Enough of subjecting Lebanon to these humiliations by negotiating directly with the Israeli enemy and listening to its dictates, and by the shameful spectacle in Washington," Qassem said.
Teheran-backed Hizbollah drew Lebanon into the Middle East conflict on March 2 by attacking Israel to avenge the killing of Iran's supreme leader, prompting waves of Israeli airstrikes and a ground invasion in the south.
Qassem expressed "gratitude" to Iran "for their support and assistance", saying the country kept the Strait of Hormuz closed until a truce was reached in Lebanon.
Iran's military declared the Strait of Hormuz closed again on Saturday, after a brief reopening, in response to a US blockade of its ports.
The Lebanese government has taken several unprecedented steps against Hizbollah over the past year, including vowing to disarm it and outlawing its military activities.
Hizbollah has rejected these moves, but Qassem maintained his group was "open to the fullest cooperation with the Lebanese authorities, embarking on a new chapter... and utilising our strengths within a national security strategy."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Help University blast victim hangs on to UK dream
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A month after regaining consciousness, the victim of an air-conditioning compressor explosion at Help University still has no recollection of the incident.
All she knows is that giving up her dream of studying in the United Kingdom is not an option.
Reeya Kaur Thandal, 19, was supposed to fly to the UK in September, but the plan had to be postponed.
"Next year, I'm going to continue my studies in the UK. I can't wait. I have a few options—Liverpool, Leeds, Sheffield or Cardiff.
"My friends have been updating me on what I've missed. There are a lot of things I missed in class," she told the New Straits Times.
Her parents told her what happened three months ago, but she has no memory of the weeks before the blast or of the fateful day.
"I read messages from a WhatsApp group where someone said it was something like an explosion in my class. I don't even remember what happened.
"I don't know if something hit me. I cannot remember anything.
I just remember waking up in the hospital," she said at a rehabilitation centre in Petaling Jaya.
Her parents said she had begun reading messages from friends, but her focus remained slow and she was not yet fully healed.
Although she could talk and carry out daily tasks, her memories of the incident and the weeks prior were still missing.
They described her as "90 per cent conscious".
Reeya is undergoing a multidisciplinary rehabilitation programme involving physiotherapy and speech and occupational therapy.
Her mother, Maneejeet Kaur Jasal, 47, is relieved to see Reeya gradually recover.
"She didn't wake up suddenly.
She opened her eyes first, then started nodding when I asked her questions.
"She was moving her hands and legs and was very restless and agitated.
It was overwhelming at first, but it's such a relief that I can communicate with her now.
"Then they removed the tracheostomy tube because the doctor realised she was trying to talk.
She was mouthing words, but no sound came out."
Maneejeet said once the tracheostomy tube — a device inserted through a surgical opening in the neck into the trachea to enable breathing — was removed, her daughter spoke throughout the night and refused to sleep.
"The first word she said was 'no' because she was in pain from the removal.
Then, for the whole night, she talked non-stop.
"I was trying to sleep and asked her to go to sleep.
Reeya replied, 'No, I want to talk.'
"When she started walking with assistance, the therapist made her walk towards me and hug me.
"It was the first time I heard her voice again and felt her hug.
I'm slowly getting her back.
"It's nice to see her eating on her own and craving her favourite food," she said.
The recovery has also brought relief to her father, Ravin Singh, 52, who works in Singapore and flies to Kuala Lumpur every week to be by his daughter's side.
As the family celebrates small milestones, the cause of the blast remains unclear.
"We have not heard anything about the forensic report," Ravin said, adding that they were still waiting for findings from the Fire and Rescue Department.
He said the university had reportedly engaged a private forensic agency to investigate the incident.
The NST has reached out to the Fire and Rescue Department, but has yet to receive a response.
Police said they received the post-mortem, chemistry and Fire and Rescue Department reports on Feb 24 and that the investigation paper had been referred to the coroner.
Reeya suffered skull fractures and bleeding in the brain after the explosion that occurred during air-conditioning maintenance work at the university on Jan 12.
The blast killed Soo Yu Juan, 24, a Universiti Tunku Abdul Rahman student who was an intern at the company carrying out the work.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Task force to pursue asset forfeiture, tax recovery in e-waste blitz
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Multi-Agency Task Force (MATF), following the various seizures of e-waste containers in the country's ports, will be zeroing in on asset forfeiture and tax recovery linked to illicit e-waste operations.
Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) Chief Commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki said this would be among the issues raised when the MATF convenes next week to further coordinate enforcement efforts under Op Hazard.
He said the high-level meeting would be held at the commission's headquarters in Putrajaya tomorrow.
"It will bring together representatives from key agencies, including the Inland Revenue Board, Customs Department, police through its Internal Security and Public Order Department, and the Department of Environment.
"The session would focus on strengthening inter-agency coordination following the latest phase of enforcement under Op Hazard 3.0, with particular attention on asset forfeiture and tax recovery linked to illicit e-waste operations," he told the New Straits Times.
Azam said closer coordination and intelligence sharing among enforcement bodies would be critical in sustaining momentum against organised syndicates involved in the e-waste trade.
"This includes aligning our intelligence and operational capabilities so that enforcement actions are more targeted and effective," he said.
He added that he was aware of ongoing efforts at the port level, including initiatives by the Port Klang Joint Task Force on E-waste led by Port Klang Malaysian Border Control and Protection Agency (MCBA) commander Datuk Nik Ezanee Mohd Faisal.
Azam said the task force's move to rope in the police's Internal Security and Public Order Department to enhance intelligence sharing and enforcement beyond port areas was a positive development.
"This kind of coordination is what we want to see, where agencies work together and share information seamlessly to ensure that enforcement is not carried out in silos," he said.
He added that the cooperation would allow the MCBA to channel information to the police to snuff out premises receiving illegal e-waste brought in via Port Klang.
Op Hazard 3.0, launched earlier this month, has seen coordinated raids nationwide targeting illegal e-waste processing facilities and related activities, with authorities intensifying efforts to dismantle syndicates and disrupt their financial networks.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Authorities plug loophole to stop repatriated e-waste containers from returning to Malaysia
Article:
PORT KLANG: Authorities have moved to plug a critical loophole in the country's fight against illegal electronic waste (e-waste) by leveraging a nationwide "selective list" system to ensure repatriated containers do not slip back into Malaysian ports.
Port Klang Malaysian Border Control and Protection Agency (MCBA) commander Datuk Nik Ezanee Mohd Faisal said the mechanism, maintained by the Customs Department, was being actively used by the Port Klang Joint Task Force on E-waste (JTF) to track high-risk containers across all ports and entry points.
"Customs will share this list across all ports and entry points nationwide and MCBA will do the same," he told the New Straits Times.
Nik Ezanee said every container repatriated under Op Green Shield would have its identification details recorded within this system, allowing authorities to immediately detect any attempt to re-enter the country.
"If these containers come back, they will be flagged straight away and subjected to thorough inspections," he said, adding that the move is aimed at stopping syndicates from exploiting routing loopholes.
The enhanced tracking comes as enforcement agencies intensify coordinated operations under the JTF, which has been spearheading large-scale inspections, seizures and repatriation of illegal e-waste shipments at Port Klang.
Nik Ezanee said the use of the selective list strengthened enforcement against so-called "U-turn" containers — shipments rejected and later rerouted back into Malaysia using different ports or falsified documentation.
Under current procedures, containers identified as e-waste are blocked, inspected and verified before shipping agents are issued a 30-day notice to repatriate them.
"Now, with the selective list being shared nationwide, any flagged container attempting to re-enter through another port can be detected early," he added.
At present, Nik Ezanee said 15 e-waste containers have been shipped out of Malaysia, marking steady progress in repatriation efforts.
On further Op Green Shield inspections, he said 208 containers have been flagged for inspection over the next few weeks.
The JTF, established on Feb 4, comprises multiple agencies including MCBA, Department of Environment, Customs Department, Sirim and port authorities, each playing a role in inspection, certification and enforcement.
MCBA has been empowered to immediately block suspicious containers and manage their release following inspections, while the Customs Department facilitates coordination with shipping lines and enforces repatriation notices.
Nik Ezanee said intelligence sharing and system integration between agencies remained critical to the success of the operation.
"This is not just about sending containers back. It is about ensuring they do not come back again using different routes or declarations.
"Our focus now is precision enforcement, identifying high-risk containers early and stopping them before they enter the system, while ensuring legitimate trade is not disrupted."
The latest measures underscore Malaysia's firm stance against illegal e-waste imports, which have been classified under prohibited items, as authorities continue efforts to dismantle syndicates using the country as a dumping ground.
It was previously reported that MCBA aimed to repatriate 150 containers of illegal e-waste by May 1, following the shipment of the first batch of 15 containers via Westports on Tuesday.
Further shipments are expected in the coming days.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran shuts Hormuz again as Trump warns of 'blackmail'
Article:
TEHERAN: Iran's military declared the Strait of Hormuz closed again on Saturday, prompting ships to abandon attempts to transit and President Donald Trump to warn Teheran against trying to "blackmail" the United States.
On Friday, Teheran had declared the strait, which usually carries a fifth of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas, open after a ceasefire was agreed in Israel's war with Iran's ally Hizbollah in Lebanon.
This prompted elation in global markets and sent oil prices plunging, but with Trump insisting that a US naval blockade of Iranian ports would continue until a deal to end the wider war was concluded, Teheran said it was shuttering the strait once more late Saturday morning.
Iran's central military command said that, in response to the US blockade, Hormuz was again "under strict management and control of the armed forces." The powerful Revolutionary Guards warned that any effort to cross "will be considered cooperation with the enemy, and the offending vessel will be targeted."
Trump had previously said the two sides were "very close" to striking a deal, but following Iran's announcement on Saturday, he insisted Teheran "can't blackmail us", even as he maintained communications were ongoing.
"We'll be talking about Iran later. We have very good conversations going on," he added, accusing Teheran of getting "a little cute" with its recent moves.
Iran's top national security body said during a visit by the military chief of mediator Pakistan that "new proposals have been put forward by the Americans, which the Islamic Republic of Iran is currently reviewing and has not yet responded to."
It went on to say, however, that Iran's negotiating delegation would not offer "even the slightest compromise, retreat or leniency" in talks with Washington.
- SHIPS THREATENED -
A handful of oil and gas tankers crossed the Strait of Hormuz early Saturday during the brief reopening, tracking data showed, but others retreated and tracking platforms showed hardly any vessels crossing the waterway by the late afternoon.
A UK maritime security agency said Iran's Revolutionary Guards fired at one tanker, while security intelligence firm Vanguard Tech reported the force had threatened to "destroy" an empty cruise ship that was fleeing the Gulf.
In a third incident, the UK agency said it received a report of a vessel in the same area "being hit by an unknown projectile which caused damage" to shipping containers but no fire.
New Delhi later summoned the Iranian ambassador to lodge a protest over a "shooting incident" involving two Indian-flagged ships in the strait, its foreign ministry said.
Speaking at a diplomatic forum in Turkey, Iranian deputy foreign minister Saeed Khatibzadeh said the "Americans cannot impose their will over Iran" with a siege, and suggested the blockade was a violation of the ceasefire that merited "repercussions."
Meanwhile, in a written message, Iran's supreme leader Mojtaba Khamenei, who has yet to be seen since taking power, said Iran's navy "stands ready" to defeat the United States.
- FOUR DAYS LEFT -
There are just four days remaining before the end of the two-week ceasefire in the US-Israel war on Iran, launched on Feb 28.
Nevertheless, Trump has appeared convinced that a deal could be finished shortly, issuing a stream of upbeat social media posts including ones praising mediator Pakistan.
Egypt, which has also been involved in diplomatic efforts, appeared similarly optimistic on Saturday, with Foreign Minister Badr Abdelatty saying Cairo and Islamabad hoped to secure a final agreement "in the coming days."
He was speaking at the same event in Antalya as Khatibzadeh, who insisted no date had been set for the next round of talks, though Teheran was "very much committed to diplomacy."
The Middle East war began with a massive wave of US-Israeli surprise attacks on Iran, despite Washington and Teheran being engaged in negotiations at the time.
The conflict rapidly spread across the region, with Iran targeting US interests in the Gulf and Hizbollah dragging Lebanon into the conflict by launching rockets at Israel.
In a sign that the two-week ceasefire remained stable, Iran's civil aviation agency declared its airspace was open again, with international flights able to transit Iran via the east of the country.
Two major sticking points in the peace talks – Iran's stockpile of near-weapons-grade enriched uranium and the future of the Strait of Hormuz – appeared up in the air.
Trump declared Friday that Iran had agreed to hand over its 440 or so kilogrammes of enriched uranium, saying: "We're going to get it by going in with Iran, with lots of excavators."
But Iran's foreign ministry had said just hours before that its stockpile, thought to be buried deep under rubble by US bombing in last June's 12-day war, was "not going to be transferred anywhere", and that surrendering it "to the US has never been raised in negotiations." --AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Israeli army demolishes homes in south Lebanon town
Article:
BEIRUT: Israeli forces on Saturday carried out demolitions in the southern Lebanese town of Bint Jbeil, the scene of intense fighting with Hizbollah prior to the recently agreed 10-day truce, Lebanese state media reported.
"The Israeli enemy is repeating its house detonating operations in the town of Bint Jbeil," Lebanon's state-run National News Agency (NNA) said, also reporting demolitions in other border towns where Israeli troops are present.
Bint Jbeil, located around five kilometres north of the Israeli border, had been the scene of heavy fighting between Israeli forces and Hizbollah for days before the truce went into force at midnight on Thursday.
The town has long been both a symbolic and strategic flashpoint in confrontations between Israel and Hizbollah.
It was the site of some of the fiercest fighting during the 2006 war, when Hizbollah's resistance there became central to the group's narrative of defiance.
And it was from the stadium in Bint Jbeil in 2000 that the group's former chief Hassan Nasrallah delivered his "Liberation" speech following Israel's withdrawal from southern Lebanon after 22 years of occupation.
Israeli Defence Minister Israel Katz has previously said house demolitions would be carried out "in order to remove once and for all the border-adjacent threats" as part of efforts to establish a security zone in south Lebanon.
His Lebanese counterpart Michel Menassa decried the plans as evidence of an intention to "forcibly displace hundreds of thousands of citizens, and systematically destroy villages."
After a November 2024 ceasefire sought to end the last conflict between Israel and Hizbollah – during which Nasrallah and other top leaders were killed – Israeli troops also carried out a series of demolitions in certain towns.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran death toll rises to 3,468 in war with US, Israel
Article:
TEHERAN: Iran's state-run Foundation of Martyrs and Veterans Affairs said on Saturday that the war with the United States and Israel had killed more than 3,400 people in the Islamic republic.
The announcement comes in the midst of a two-week ceasefire in the conflict, which erupted in late February with US-Israeli strikes on Teheran.
Foundation head Ahmad Mousavi was quoted by the ISNA news agency as saying that 3,468 "martyrs... fell during the recent conflict."
A previous toll from the head of the Iranian Legal Medicine Organisation issued on April 12 said 3,375 people in Iran had been killed in the war.
The US-based Human Rights Activists News Agency (HRANA) said on April 7 that at least 3,636 people had been killed, including 1,701 civilians – among them at least 254 children – as well as 1,221 military personnel and 714 people whose status had not been classified.
Due to reporting restrictions, AFP is not able to access the sites of strikes nor to independently verify tolls in Iran.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Israel sets Gaza-style 'Yellow Line' in southern Lebanon
Article:
JERUSALEM: Israel's military said Saturday it had established a "Yellow Line" in southern Lebanon, similar to the one separating its forces from Hamas-held areas in Gaza, adding it had hit a "fighter cell" operating along the demarcation.
Since a ceasefire in Gaza came into effect in October, the boundary has divided the Palestinian territory into military- and Hamas-controlled zones, with Israeli troops routinely firing on anyone approaching it.
In its first mention of such a demarcation in Lebanon, the army said Saturday that its troops in the south "identified fighters who violated the ceasefire understandings and approached the forces from north of the Yellow Line in a manner that posed an immediate threat."
"In order to eliminate the threat... forces attacked the fighters in several areas," it added, noting that the military was authorised to act against imminent threats in spite of the recently agreed ceasefire in Lebanon.
In a separate statement later in the day, the military said the air force had eliminated a "fighters' cell operating in proximity to IDF soldiers in southern Lebanon, in the area of the forward defence line", referring to the Yellow Line.
It did not specify how many suspected fighters were killed.
The army said it also "struck an underground shaft in the area south of the forward defence line, as well as Hizbollah fighters who were identified entering it."
Israel and Lebanon agreed to a 10-day ceasefire on Thursday to allow for negotiations to end six weeks of war between Israel and the Iran-backed group Hizbollah.
The war saw massive Israeli airstrikes across Lebanon and a ground invasion in the south.
Lebanese authorities say the war has killed nearly 2,300 people since it began on March 2, and had caused widespread devastation in southern towns and cities such as Nabatiyeh.
Hizbollah halted military operations after the ceasefire came into effect, but warned that it was keeping its "finger on the trigger" in case Israel violated it.
- 'CRUCIAL' TALKS -
Lebanese President Joseph Aoun said on Friday that direct negotiations with Israel "are crucial", and that the government aims to "consolidate a ceasefire (and) secure the withdrawal of Israeli forces from the occupied southern territories."
Hizbollah strongly opposes the planned negotiations between the two sides, which have technically been at war for decades.
US President Donald Trump has said Washington "prohibited" Israel from bombing Lebanon following the ceasefire deal, and that the US would work with Lebanon to "deal with" Hizbollah.
But Israel has not "yet finished the job" against the group, Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said, vowing to press on "dismantling" it just hours after the truce came into effect.
Since before the latest war, Beirut has pledged to disarm Hizbollah and assert its authority as the sole legal bearer of arms across the country, but Israel had criticised its efforts as insufficient.
Lebanese army chief Rodolphe Haykal visited the south on Saturday to review his troops' "operational situation" in Kfar Dunin after the implementation of the ceasefire.
He told soldiers there that "the Lebanese people look to (the army) during this difficult period", even as he acknowledged its "limited resources."
Kfar Dunin is located around four kilometres away from where a French United Nations peacekeeper was killed earlier on Saturday, with Paris accusing Hizbollah of carrying out the attack, which it denied.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran Guards warn ships nearing Hormuz will be targeted
Article:
TEHERAN: The navy of Iran's Revolutionary Guards warned on Saturday that any ship approaching the Strait of Hormuz would be treated as a target.
"We warn that no ship, of any kind, should leave its anchorage in the Persian Gulf and the Sea of Oman. Any attempt to approach the Strait of Hormuz will be considered cooperation with the enemy, and the offending vessel will be targeted," said the statement published on the Guards' official Sepah News website.  --AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: Stranded rider vows to repay mechanic's kindness
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A motorcyclist who went viral after being
helped by a stranger has vowed to repay
the mechanic who came to his aid during a late-night breakdown.
The rider, known only as Dicky, said he was deeply moved by the public response after his story spread on TikTok, which highlighted the mechanic's decision to assist him without taking payment.
"I will look for him next week. To those who have given me gifts on TikTok LIVE, I will pass the money to him so he can help others," he said.
The incident,
which has drawn widespread attention online,
stemmed from a late-night encounter where a local mechanic chose compassion over profit when responding to the stranded rider.
The mechanic, who runs a workshop in Taman Kota Damansara, said he had closed early due to heavy rain when he received a call at about 12.30am from a man who had found his contact details via TikTok.
"He told me he had called other mechanics, but they wouldn't help him; one even blocked his number. His motorcycle chain had snapped, and the front sprocket was missing. He was stranded and had nowhere else to turn," the mechanic said.
The mechanic initially quoted RM145 for the emergency repair, covering parts and labour. The rider, however, said he could only afford RM80.
Although hesitant at first, the mechanic agreed to assist and travelled to the location. Upon arrival, he learned the rider had borrowed money from a friend to settle the repair.
As the work was completed, the rider attempted to make payment. However, the mechanic noticed the balance on the man's phone and realised it was his last savings.
"He kept insisting that I take the money for my work. But seeing his situation, I just couldn't do it," he said.
The mechanic then refused payment.
The story has since gone viral, with many netizens praising the mechanic's compassion and willingness to help a stranger in need.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police kill shooter in Kyiv, president says five people dead
Article:
KYIV: Ukrainian police killed a man who opened fire in a district of Kyiv and barricaded himself into a supermarket on Saturday in an incident that killed at least five people. President Volodymyr Zelensky, writing on the Telegram messaging app, quoted Interior Minister Ihor Klymenko as saying that five people had been killed in the city's Holosiivskyi district and 10 were being treated in hospital.
Mayor Vitali Klitschko, also writing on Telegram, said a woman among the 10 injured had died in hospital.
"The shooter in Kyiv was liquidated during the arrest," Klymenko said on Telegram.
"Special forces of the... national police stormed the store where the attacker was. He took people hostage and shot at a policeman during his detention. Before that, negotiators tried to contact him."
Prosecutor-General Ruslan Kravchenko said the shooter had been identified as a 58-year-old native of Moscow and a fire had broken out in the Kyiv apartment where the suspect was registered.
Kravchenko said four people had been killed in the street and one inside the supermarket, where the suspect had brandished an automatic weapon.
He posted a photo showing a blurred prone figure covered in blood inside a store, a weapon lying nearby. — REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran says reviewing 'new proposals' from US
Article:
TEHERAN: Iran's top national security body said Saturday that the country was reviewing "new proposals" received from the US, even as it warned that its negotiators would cut no compromises with Washington.
"In recent days, with the presence of the commander of the Pakistani army in Teheran as an intermediary and mediator in the negotiations, new proposals have been put forward by the Americans, which the Islamic Republic of Iran is currently reviewing and has not yet responded to," the Supreme National Security Council said in a statement carried by state media.
It went on to say that Iran's negotiating delegation "will not make even the slightest compromise, retreat or leniency, and will defend with all its strength the interests of the Iranian nation."
Pakistan has emerged as the main mediator between the two foes, and hosted an initial round of talks last week that ended without an agreement.
The disclosure of the new proposals came hours after Iran declared that it was reimposing its closure of the Strait of Hormuz over an ongoing US blockade of its ports, prompting a warning from US President Donald Trump not to try to "blackmail" Washington.
The Supreme National Security Council said "Iran is determined to enforce supervision and control over traffic through the Strait of Hormuz until the definitive end of the war", insisting the US blockade would preclude even a "conditional and limited reopening."
The council said such a reopening would involve Teheran issuing transit certificates to vessels, "as well as requiring payment of fees related to services for security, safety and environmental protection." — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Spirit of goodwill, media collaboration must be strengthened - Bernama chairman
Article:
MELAKA: The spirit of goodwill and cooperation between the media and community organisations must continue to be strengthened in addressing current challenges, including the spread of misinformation and global uncertainties.
Malaysian National News Agency (Bernama) Chairman Datuk Seri Wong Chun Wai said the media plays a significant role not only as a conveyor of news but also in shaping public perception, fostering empathy, and connecting communities from diverse backgrounds.
"The media does not merely deliver news, but shapes perceptions, nurtures empathy and connects society," he said when speaking at the Media Appreciation Night organised by the Melaka Civil Defence Force Associate Officers Association (PPBAPM) here last night.
He said media coverage of Melaka PPBAPM's various initiatives, including humanitarian missions, emergency response and community training, has helped raise public awareness and strengthen the spirit of togetherness among society.
Meanwhile, Wong said the world today faces multiple challenges, including humanitarian crises and the spread of misinformation, which require a more responsible approach from all parties.
"Goodwill is not merely a slogan, but a commitment to mutual understanding, respect and cooperation despite differences in background," he said.
He also praised Melaka PPBAPM's commitment, which was not only active operationally but also consistently promoted racial harmony through inclusive, community-based initiatives.
"Such approaches are the best examples of how goodwill can be translated into real action," he said.
Wong added that society today needed healthy spaces for dialogue and unifying narratives, stressing that all parties, including the media, uniformed bodies, the corporate sector and the public, shared the responsibility of ensuring that every message conveyed is constructive.
"With close cooperation and trust, we can build a more harmonious, resilient and united society," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sultan of Pahang urges caution during water activities in high-risk areas
Article:
KUANTAN: The Sultan of Pahang, Al-Sultan Abdullah Ri'ayatuddin Al-Mustafa Billah Shah, and the Tengku Ampuan of Pahang, Tunku Azizah Aminah Maimunah Iskandariah, have urged the public to remain vigilant when engaging in water activities, particularly in high-risk areas such as beaches, rivers and recreational water sites.
Their Royal Highnesses also stressed the importance of prioritising safety, including wearing life jackets when at sea or in open waters, and avoiding unsupervised or unsafe activities.
"Parents and guardians are also urged to closely monitor the movements and safety of their children, especially near water, to prevent any untoward incidents," they said in a post on the Pahang Sultanate's Facebook page tonight.
They said that vigilance, situational awareness and strict adherence to safety measures could help reduce the risk of accidents and ultimately save lives.
Al-Sultan Abdullah and Tunku Azizah also conveyed their condolences to the families of two students, Luqman Kamil Jamaluddin, 16, and Muhammad Fauzan Aziman Mohd Saad, 18, who were found drowned in separate incidents in Kuantan and Maran today.
They prayed that the souls of the deceased be placed among the righteous and expressed hope that their families would be granted strength and ease in facing their loss.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Two killed in separate crashes in Perak
Article:
IPOH: Two men were killed in separate incidents at Km281.1 (southbound) of the North-South Expressway (PLUS) and in Kampung Sungai Ranggam, Teluk Intan, today.
District police chief Assistant Commissioner Muhammad Najib Hamzah said in the first incident this morning, a 21-year-old motorcyclist was killed after colliding with a lorry.
"Preliminary investigations found that a lorry driven by a 54-year-old man was travelling in the left lane from Bercham towards Kuala Lumpur.
"The motorcycle, which was travelling in the same lane from behind, is believed to have lost control and was unable to avoid the collision, crashing into the lorry," he said in a statement.
He said the motorcyclist sustained severe injuries and was pronounced dead at the scene. The body was sent to Raja Permaisuri Bainun Hospital for a post-mortem.
Investigations revealed that the victim was part of a motorcycle convoy travelling from Nibong Tebal to Sungkai.
"Both vehicles have been detained for inspection by Puspakom, and the lorry driver will undergo a urine test to assist in investigations," he said, adding that the case was being investigated under Section 41(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987.
In a separate incident, a 25-year-old Indonesian man was killed after the mini tractor he was driving overturned into a drain behind a mosque in Kampung Sungai Ranggam.
Perak Fire and Rescue Department operations division assistant director Sabarozi Nor Ahmad said the department received a call at 1.51pm.
"Upon arrival, police were already at the scene. The incident involved a mini tractor used to transport oil palm fruit bunches.
"The victim had been trapped but was extricated by members of the public before Fire and Rescue personnel arrived," he said.
He said the victim was pronounced dead at the scene and his body was handed over to the police for further action.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fadhlina extends condolences to the family of drowned teen
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Education Minister Fadhlina Sidek has conveyed her condolences to the family of Luqman Kamil Jamaluddin, 16, who was found drowned after being reported missing while searching for clams at Balok Beach, Pahang, yesterday.
In a Facebook post today, Fadhlina said the Education Ministry's (MoE) immediate priority was to ensure that appropriate assistance is extended to the family.
"My condolences go out to the entire family. May the deceased's soul be placed among the faithful, and may his family be granted strength and patience in facing this difficult ordeal," she said.
In the incident yesterday evening, Luqman Kamil, a Form Four student at Sekolah Menengah Kebangsaan Sultan Abu Bakar, Kuantan, was believed to have drowned while searching for clams at the beach.
Pahang Fire and Rescue Department operations assistant director Mohd Salahuddin Isa said in a statement that the victim's body was found at 2.15pm, floating about 400 metres from the estuary, by the search and rescue team, which had been conducting operations since yesterday.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sanchez emerges as progressive star against Trump chaos
Article:
Spain's Pedro Sanchez hosted a summit of world progressives last week with greater global clout as turbulent relations with US President Donald Trump bolster his image as a left-wing hero.
Clashes with Trump, virulent criticism of Israel and a championing of immigration have set the Socialist prime minister apart in Europe, which has in the last years tilted to the right.
The latest episode was his staunch opposition to the US-Israeli war on Iran, with Trump threatening trade retaliation after Spain denied the use of its bases.
Sanchez broke with Nato allies last year by refusing to agree to Trump's demand that alliance members hike defence spending to five per cent of GDP.
He is also the highest-profile Western leader to call Israel's devastating two-year war against Hamas in Gaza a "genocide".
For Ignacio Molina, a senior fellow at Madrid's Elcano Royal Institute, Sanchez has earned Spain recognition for a "coherent" stance in the Global South, particularly in the Arab world and Latin America.
"It works out well for the government, because it has gained a lot of leadership, influence and presence in many countries," Molina said.
Of the countries adopting a similar stance, Spain is the "most relevant" because others are not in Nato, such as Ireland, or outside the European Union, for example Norway, he added, citing nations who also recognised a Palestinian state in 2024.
"Spain has achieved a weight among the European Union's big countries that it did not have before," agreed Joan Botella, a political science professor at the Autonomous University of Barcelona.
Sanchez has gained attention in international media, penning articles for the New York Times and Le Monde diplomatique.
"Pedro Sanchez has become the standard-bearer for Western political opposition to the US president," The Wall Street Journal wrote in March, while the Financial Times called the Socialist "Trump's nemesis in Europe".
Bathing in the new-found limelight, the current president of Socialist International hosted leading leftist figures at the two-day Global Progressive Mobilisation in Barcelona on Friday.
Brazil's Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, South African President Cyril Ramaphosa and Mexico's Claudia Sheinbaum attended alongside 400 mayors and more than 100 parties.
Sanchez and Lula delivered the keynote address at the gathering, which organisers say aims to rally progressives in times of turmoil marked by the rise of the far right.
Progressives must "unite, tell citizens that we belong to something that goes beyond domestic politics, our borders — and that is having a positive, humanist outlook," Sanchez said on Tuesday during his fourth visit to China in as many years.
The rise of Sanchez's stock abroad contrasts with his polarising image at home.
He has never commanded a parliamentary majority since taking office in 2018 and is under pressure from corruption investigations into relatives and former close political allies.
Botella said Sanchez was "playing the foreign policy card hard, because it's an area he's comfortable in, and in which a majority of Spanish public opinion is favourable to him".
More than 68 per cent of Spaniards have opposed the war on Iran, including voters of the conservative main opposition Popular Party (PP), according to a March poll published in El Pais newspaper.
"Spaniards have a certain inferiority complex when they go out into the wider world. In that sense, the profile that Sanchez's figure has acquired satisfies many people beyond his electoral base," Botella said.
On the other hand, the PP says he has used foreign policy exclusively for domestic purposes, to rally fractious left-wing forces and distract attention from the negative headlines.
Other Western leaders have preferred to handle Trump with tact on trade, defence and foreign policy.
Sanchez "is trying to use this image of a progressive leader, opposed to Trump" to "strengthen his political position" and "absorb the left-wing vote", said Juan Tovar Ruiz, a professor of international relations at the University of Burgos.
"That has consequences at European level. Right now, I think Spain is in a clearly minority position," he warned.
For Molina, Sanchez's stance risked alienating some traditional allies governed by the right, such as Germany and Italy, but "in the end, what is gained is rather more than what may be lost."
The writer is from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RM28.8mil allocated to boost Malaysia's halal industry under 13MP
Article:
KUANTAN: A total of RM28.8 million has been allocated under the First Rolling Plan of the 13th Malaysia Plan (13MP) for projects and programmes to develop the halal industry, says Deputy Economy Minister Datuk Mohd Shahar Abdullah.
He said the allocation this year had been channelled to the Entrepreneur Development and Cooperatives Ministry, the Department of Islamic Development Malaysia (Jakim) and the Halal Development Corporation (HDC).
Among the approved initiatives are programmes focusing on halal innovation and technology development, facilitation of halal certification, and strengthening halal integrity, including the enhancement of Jakim's My e-Halal system.
"With the funding and financing support provided, halal industry players are expected to build their business capacity and, in turn, contribute to the development of the national halal industry," he said.
Shahar was speaking at the Pahang International East Coast Entrepreneurial Summit (Pieces), held in conjunction with the Malaysia East Coast Trade and Entrepreneur Expo 2026 here today.
Speaking to Bernama separately, he said Pahang had strong potential to further develop its halal industry, given its strategic location and logistical advantages.
He said industry players should be ready to leverage infrastructure such as the East Coast Rail Link (ECRL), which is expected to be operational next year.
"The facilities are already in place, and the industry must be developed as quickly as possible. The Economy Ministry sees that Pahang has its strengths, but we need synergy — no more working in silos.
"Most importantly, we must be aggressive in seizing available opportunities so that they do not go to waste, especially for local youth and entrepreneurs," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: The dark side of art that fuels money laundering
Article:
The very nature of art makes it an attractive vehicle for money laundering.
It is particularly vulnerable because art can be easily concealed, privately traded and assigned highly subjective values, enabling criminals to convert illicit funds into apparently legitimate assets.
Global money laundering is estimated to range between US$600 billion and US$1.5 trillion annually, accounting for about two to five per cent of global GDP.
According to research highlighted by the United Nations Office on Drug and Crime, theft and black-market activities linked to stolen art alone are estimated to generate US$6 billion each year.
The three stages of artwork-based money laundering unfold in distinct ways. During the placement stage, illicit funds are used to purchase artwork through private sellers or online platforms.
In the layering stage, the artwork is resold multiple times through intermediaries or stored in free ports with relatively low risk of detection.
Finally, in the integration stage, the piece is sold through a reputable auction house, allowing the proceeds to re-enter the financial system as seemingly legitimate income.
In Malaysia, while the art market is not a primary channel for money laundering, it remains a high-opacity sector that may attract sophisticated financial criminals. Another factor is weak controls in certain segments.
For example, the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) announced on Tuesday the recovery of four high-value artworks linked to the 1MDB scandal, valued at RM786,556.
The works, which include pieces attributed to Pablo Picasso and Joan Miro, are currently held at the MACC headquarters for authentication by the National Visual Arts Development Board.
Reports indicate that the artworks were owned by Jasmine Loo, a former legal officer at 1MDB, with confirmation from the United States Department of Justice linking them to the wider scandal.
Money launderers exploit several structural weaknesses in the art market, including private sales, the use of middlemen, anonymous deals and storing artworks in offshore free ports where ownership can hidden.
They may also use unregulated dealers, small galleries and online platforms that are not closely monitored. These allow illicit transactions to occur within or alongside legitimate art trade networks.
Money launderers use art as a single artwork can be worth a very large amount, allowing them to hide large sums of illegal money in one purchase. Unlike stocks or property, art does not have a fixed price.
Its value depends on opinions, history and how it is presented in the market, making it easier to raise or lower prices in order to disguise illegal money movements.
Furthermore, many transactions occur privately, allowing buyers and sellers to remain anonymous through intermediaries such as brokers, advisers, or shell companies, which reduces transparency and complicates regulatory oversight.
Artworks are also bought and sold on paper several times without actually being moved, which can make their price look higher and hide where the money came from.
In other cases, artworks are kept for a long time in free ports or private collections, allowing illegal money to be stored until it is later brought back into the financial system.
Reports from the Centre for Art Law and Citywealth show that illegal artworks and stolen cultural objects are sometimes stored or traded in private, low-tax, and lightly regulated free ports in places like Geneva, Luxembourg, Singapore and New York.
These storage sites allow owners to keep their identity hidden while the artworks are held outside normal customs control.
Citywealth says the Geneva Freeport is one of the biggest and oldest secure storage sites in the world.
It is about 150,000 square metres in size, and around 40 per cent of what is kept there is art.
The value of the items stored there has been estimated at US$100 billion.
Although not a major hub, Malaysia still faces potential risks, as shown in cases linked to major corruption scandals such as 1MDB. This demonstrates how art can be exploited within broader global laundering networks.
Addressing these vulnerabilities requires stronger regulation, improved transparency in ownership and valuation, and closer international cooperation.
Without such measures, the art market will continue to be an attractive avenue for disguising illicit financial flows.
The writer is professor and director at Institute of Criminology and Criminal Justice, HELP University
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Some tankers cross Strait of Hormuz before shots fired
Article:
OSLO: More than a dozen tankers, including three sanctioned vessels, passed through the Strait of Hormuz after a 50-day blockade was lifted on Friday, shipping data showed, before Iran reimposed restrictions on Saturday and fired at some vessels.
Reopening the strait is key for Gulf producers to resume full oil and gas supplies to the world, and end what the International Energy Agency has called the worst-ever supply disruption.
US President Donald Trump said on Friday Iran had agreed to open the strait, while Iranian officials said they wanted the US to fully lift its blockade of Iranian tankers.
Western shipping companies cautiously welcomed the announcements but said more clarity was needed, including on the presence of sea mines, before their vessels could transit.
IRAN RESUMES RESTRICTIONS
The ships that passed through the strait on Friday and Saturday via Iranian waters south of Larak island were mainly older, non-Western-owned vessels and included four sanctioned ships, according to ship-tracking data.
Iran arranged passage for a limited number of oil tankers and commercial ships following prior agreements in negotiations, a spokesperson for Iran's Revolutionary Guards said.
Other ships have been seen approaching the strait and turning back as Iran said it would maintain strict controls as long as the US continues its blockade of Iranian ports.
The UK Navy reported on Saturday that Iranian gunboats fired at some ships attempting to cross the strait.
Some merchant vessels received radio messages from Iran's navy saying the strait was shut again and that no ships were allowed to pass, shipping sources said on Saturday.
Ship-tracking data showed five vessels loaded with liquefied natural gas from Ras Laffan in Qatar approaching the strait on Saturday morning.
No LNG cargoes have transited the waterway since the US-Israeli war with Iran began on Feb 28.
Hundreds of ships have been stuck in the Gulf since the conflict started and Teheran closed the strait, forcing Gulf oil and gas producers to sharply cut production.
Top producers such as Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Iraq and Kuwait say they need steady tanker flows and unrestricted passage through the strait to resume normal export operations.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NST Leader: How to put idle federal land to good use
Article:
Idle federal land has long been an issue for Putrajaya.
Being blessed with good times for most of its 69 years of independence, Malaysia's successive administration has not felt the economic cost of leaving its land undeveloped.
But these are tough economic times that compel the use of every hectare of the country's land bank for the well-being of its people.
Prices are on the surge, from fuel to fertiliser to food.
The war in Iran in the Middle East isn't just an attack on a distant land; it is, as economists say, an attack on the global economy.
They are right.
Just look at the epic consequences of the fury of the bombs the world over.
The unfortunate fact is this: those who started the illegal war have left the rest of the world to save their own economy.
Putrajaya's move to re-examine undeveloped federal land is a step in the right direction.
A little late in the day, but nevertheless a welcome move.
Because idle land represents lost economic opportunities (agriculture, housing, industry); foregone government revenue; and increased exposure to illegal occupation, encroachment and environmental degradation.
But re-examining undeveloped federal land should not end merely with a report of a committee.
It must lead to a cultural shift in how public assets are managed — from passive ownership to active stewardship.
The proposal to form a special committee to coordinate federal land use is a step forward but risks becoming another layer of bureaucracy unless properly empowered.
To be effective, such a body must conduct a comprehensive, nationwide audit of federal land; set clear utilisation targets and timelines; have the authority to reassign or reclaim underutilised land; and report regularly to Parliament and the public.
We recommend five ways to make the cultural shift happen.
Firstly, a centralised federal land registry with real-time data must be established.
We must not forget that we are operating in a data-driven world.
Like it or not, we are not good with data, let alone real-time data.
This Leader's search for the size of our federal idle land bank turned up empty.
As the management adage goes, if you can't measure it you can't manage it.
The next step is to turn the numbers into data-in-motion for real-time analysis and decision-making.
The introduction of mandatory utilisation plans for all land holdings is our second recommendation.
Thirdly, senior civil service KPIs must be tied to asset optimisation.
This allows goals to be set, strategy to be crafted and results to be assessed.
Fourthly, we recommend that the government open selected land parcels to public-private partnerships with strict safeguards.
A caveat needs to be entered here.
"Partnerships" must not be interpreted as sale.
All this will be of no use unless the government ensures public disclosure, our final recommendation.
This is the only way for the government to build trust among the people and deter misuse of public assets.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump says Iran cannot 'blackmail us' with Strait of Hormuz
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump on Saturday warned Iran not to "blackmail" Washington with its flip-flopping on the fate of the Strait of Hormuz, after Teheran declared the strategic waterway once again closed.
"We're talking to them. They wanted to close up the strait again – you know, as they've been doing for years – and they can't blackmail us," Trump said at a White House event.
Trump said there would be "some information" about Iran later in the day, adding: "We're taking a tough stand."
Iran's military on Saturday declared the strait, through which about one-fifth of the world's crude and liquefied natural gas normally passes, was once again closed, a day after saying it was open. — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: First group of Malaysian haj pilgrims safely arrive in Madinah
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The first group of Malaysian haj pilgrims for the 1447H/2026 season safely arrived at Prince Mohammad Abdulaziz International Airport in Madinah at 8.21am local time (1.21pm Malaysian time) today.
All 284 pilgrims departed on Malaysia Airlines flight MH8050 from Kuala Lumpur International Airport (KLIA) at 4.09am (Malaysian time) and arrived at Hotel Emaar Royal in Madinah at 9.45am (local time).
Lembaga Tabung Haji (TH), in a statement today, said the group was received by Malaysia's Ambassador to Saudi Arabia Datuk Syed Mohamad Bakri Syed Abdul Rahman, Deputy Head of the Malaysian Haj Delegation Mohd Ridzuan Wahi, Malaysia's Haj Consul in Jeddah Radin Adi Hilmi, as well as TH senior management and staff.
Syed Mohamad Bakri said all preparations had been comprehensively carried out to ensure pilgrims could focus fully on their worship throughout their stay in the Holy Land.
[[nid:1420811]]
"On behalf of the Malaysian government and TH, I would like to express gratitude for the safe arrival of the first group of Malaysian haj pilgrims in Madinah. This is the result of meticulous planning, close cooperation with the Saudi authorities, and TH's continuous commitment to ensuring the welfare and well-being of pilgrims remain a priority.
"I also see that TH personnel are committed to ensuring that every pilgrim is not only comfortable in terms of welfare, but also guided towards achieving a mabrur haj, God willing," he said.
He also reminded pilgrims to take care of their health, increase their acts of worship and comply with all guidelines throughout their stay in the Holy Land.
During their six-day stay in Madinah, the pilgrims will follow a scheduled programme including visits to the Prophet's Mosque (Masjid Nabawi), the tomb of Prophet Muhammad, Raudhah and other historical sites.
TH said two more flights carrying Malaysian pilgrims were expected to arrive in Madinah today, with 284 pilgrims at 2.10pm and another 357 at 9.10pm local time.
The 1447H/2026 haj operations, which officially began today, will see 31,600 Malaysian pilgrims travel in stages to Madinah and Makkah via 100 chartered flights operated by AMAL by Malaysia Airlines and Saudia Airlines.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Govt assures B20 biodiesel safe for vehicles, no compatibility issues
Article:
PORT KLANG: The government has given assurances that there are no compatibility issues arising from the use of biodiesel, particularly at B20 levels, following the implementation of engine specifications supporting the fuel since 2020.
Economy Minister Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir said that with the standards made mandatory, the use of biodiesel should not cause damage to vehicles.
"In fact, several areas in the country have already been using B20, including Langkawi in Kedah, Labuan and Sarawak, except for Bintulu. This shows that, so far, any complaints or concerns have not reached a level that can be used as a reference.
"The government made it mandatory in 2020 for engines and vehicles to be compatible with B20, which means the industry and vehicle owners were given sufficient time to prepare," he said.
He said this at a press conference after visiting the KL-Kepong Oleomas Sdn Bhd oleochemical plant together with Plantation and Commodities Minister Datuk Seri Dr Noraini Ahmad today.
Earlier, Akmal Nasrullah said that the government had agreed to increase the biodiesel blend from B10 to B15, starting with B12, at no additional cost, in response to developments in the Middle East.
He said the move was aimed at ensuring longer-term diesel supply stability, reducing reliance on imported fossil fuels and strengthening energy security.
He added that economic restructuring through the transition to renewable energy must be accelerated.
Meanwhile, Noraini said under the 13th Malaysia Plan (13MP), RM42 million had been allocated to upgrade four palm oil refinery depots, further strengthening efforts to ensure the sustainability of the country's energy resources.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd
